coredata.dc:title: Copper selenides controlled hydrothermal synthesis of porous micro-networks with highly efficient photocatalysis
coredata.dc:creator: Shu, Q.
Qiu, W.
Luo, M.
Xiao, L.
coredata.dc:description: Hybrid photocatalysts have important prospects in clean energy and sewage treatment. Herein, we construct functional hybrid materials consisting of nonstoichiometric copper selenides (Cu2-xSe) and porous gadolinium micro-networks (P-MNs) formed by the hydrothermal treatment of gadolinium (III) nitrate and citric acid (Cu2-xSe/P-MNs) as the excellent photocatalyst for the photodegradation of fluorescent dyes. Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts show good chemical stability because the hybridization of Cu2-xSe and P-MNs is based on an in-situ growth strategy. More importantly, due to the strong light-harvesting and good electron transfer ability, Cu2-xSe/P-MNs show superior photocatalytic activity with a catalytic rate of up to 0.33 min−1 only under the irradiation of 20 W of low-power white light, which is at least two orders of magnitude higher than other common photocatalysts. Detailed studies show that the h+, O2 •−, and ⋅OH are the main active species in the photocatalytic process. According to the ‘three channel’ catalytic path, rhodamine 6G as the model dye can be completely and rapidly degraded within 15 min. Because of the good photocatalytic activity, Cu2-xSe/P-MNs show great application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu2-xSe
Rare earth micro-network
In-situ synthesis
Hybrid photocatalysts
Dye degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photodegradation process of R6G using Cu2-xSe/P-MNs
Preparation and characterization of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts
Composition and structure of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts
Photocatalytic activity of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs under white light

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts. (a, b) SEM images, (c, d) high-resolution SEM images and (e) elemental mapping of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs.

Elemental and structural characterization of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts. (a) XPS, (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) Gd3d, (e) Gd4d, (f) Cu2p, (g) Se3d, (h) FT-IR spectra and (i) thermogravimetric curve of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs.

Photocatalytic activities of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs. (a) Absorption spectra of Cu2-xSe and Cu2-xSe/P-MNs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs and S-MNs. EX: 360 nm. (c) Photodegradation of R6G under different catalytic conditions (1, S-MNs under the 20 W white light irradiation; 2, Cu2-xSe/P-MNs (50 mg) in the dark; 3, Cu2-xSe (0.2 mg/mL, 5 mL) in the dark; 4, Cu2-xSe (0.2 mg/mL, 5 mL) under the 20 W white light irradiation and 5, Cu2-xSe/P-MNs (50 mg) under the 20 W white light irradiation). R6G: 20 μM. F represents the fluorescence intensity in different photodegradation time. F0 represents the fluorescence intensity at the beginning of photodegradation reaction. EX: 490 nm. (d) Kinetics of photocatalytic process.

Photocatalytic mechanisms of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs. (a) Degradation efficiency of R6G on Cu2-xSe/P-MNs by introducing different scavengers. ESR spectra of radical adducts trapped by DMPO (b) O2 •− and (c) ⋅OH in Cu2-xSe/P-MNs. (d) Schematic diagram of the ‘three channels’ photodegradation mechanism of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs.
Schematic illustration of ‘three channel’ photocatalytic process of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs under the irradiation of white light (20 W).
Comparison of photocatalytic efficiency of dyes using different photocatalysts. RB represents rhodamine B. MB represents methylene blue. MO represents methyl orange. ARS represents alizarin red S.

head.ce:title: Copper selenides controlled hydrothermal synthesis of porous micro-networks with highly efficient photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Institute of Technology, Guiyang, 550003, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Institute of Technology, Guiyang, 550003, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hybrid photocatalysts have important prospects in clean energy and sewage treatment. Herein, we construct functional hybrid materials consisting of nonstoichiometric copper selenides (Cu2-xSe) and porous gadolinium micro-networks (P-MNs) formed by the hydrothermal treatment of gadolinium (III) nitrate and citric acid (Cu2-xSe/P-MNs) as the excellent photocatalyst for the photodegradation of fluorescent dyes. Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts show good chemical stability because the hybridization of Cu2-xSe and P-MNs is based on an in-situ growth strategy. More importantly, due to the strong light-harvesting and good electron transfer ability, Cu2-xSe/P-MNs show superior photocatalytic activity with a catalytic rate of up to 0.33 min−1 only under the irradiation of 20 W of low-power white light, which is at least two orders of magnitude higher than other common photocatalysts. Detailed studies show that the h+, O2 •−, and ⋅OH are the main active species in the photocatalytic process. According to the ‘three channel’ catalytic path, rhodamine 6G as the model dye can be completely and rapidly degraded within 15 min. Because of the good photocatalytic activity, Cu2-xSe/P-MNs show great application prospects.

Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid photocatalysts are used for the efficient photodegradation of R6G dyes under the irradiation of 20 W of low-power white light based on the ‘three channel’ catalytic path.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis is regarded as a very promising strategy to solve the growing global energy demand and increasing environmental pollution [1]. This advanced technology takes sunlight as a renewable energy source and fully meets the concept of green chemistry, which is of great significance for the production of clean energy, the degradation of environmental pollutants, and the realization of carbon neutralization. Highly efficient photocatalysis usually requires stable photocatalysts with superior performances, such as a wide light absorption range covering the whole visible region, effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, strong light absorption ability, etc. However, traditional photocatalysts such as TiO2, BiOX (X = Cl, Br and I), and Bi2WO6 have been limited for the photocatalysis due to the fast charge recombination and weak light absorption and usually exhibit the good photocatalytic activity only under ultraviolet (UV) light [2–4].

Recently, hybrid photocatalysts by integrating different nanostructures have gained considerable attention because of their unique advantages [5]. In comparison to single-component photocatalysts, hybrid photocatalysts can effectively overcome the shortcomings of single-component catalysts and significantly improve the overall catalytic performance. For example, Di et al. [6] prepare carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/Bi2WO6 hybrid materials for the photocatalytic degradation of bisphenol A. The hybridization of CQDs and Bi2WO6 enhances the light harvesting ability of photocatalysts and forms more electron–hole pairs resulting in an increased photocatalytic activity. Li et al. [7] construct carbon dots (CDs)/TiO2 nanocomposites for the photoreduction of Cr(VI) under sunlight irradiation. CDs/TiO2 nanocomposites show excellent photoreduction performance because of the upconversion and electron transfer properties of CDs. Liu et al. [8] synthesize metal-complex/semiconductor (Ru-BNAH/TiO2/Cu2O) hybrid catalysts for efficient CO2 reduction. The efficient photoelectrocatalytic activity of hybrid catalysts is mainly attributed to two aspects: (i) excellent proton coupling of the bio-proton carrier (Ru-BNAH) and (ii) the photosensitivity of the metal oxide substrate (TiO2/Cu2O). Moreover, some other hybrid photocatalysts such as Zn3(VO4)2/Zn2V2O7/ZnO ternary heteronanostructure [9], fluorine-doped Sn3O4 [10], core-shell like Ag2S@Ag2CO3 [11], CuS/TiO2 architectures [12], Cu2-xSe/CdS composite [13], Cu2-xSe/BiVO4 composite [14], Cu2-xSe/TiO2 composite [15], Bi2WO6/Ta3N5 heterojunction [16], TaON/Bi2MoO6 core–shell heterojunction [17], Bi2Sn2O7/Bi2MoO6 heterojunction [18], Ag/Ag2S/Bi2MoO6 plasmonic heterojunction [19], and tetra (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin/Bi2MoO6 composites [20] have also been developed for the efficient photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. More importantly, the intimate interface between two components of a hybrid photocatalyst can effectively improve the separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs [21–24], causing the higher photocatalysis activity compared with a single-component photocatalyst.

Although hybrid photocatalysts have great potential applications in the field of catalysis, there are still some problems that make it difficult to realize industrialized photocatalytic conversion under sunlight. For example, most hybrid photocatalysts still need hundreds of watts of white light to have an ideal photocatalytic activity [25–27], but this not only increases the cost but also is far from realizing high-efficiency photocatalysis under sunlight. Meanwhile, hybrid photocatalysts prepared by physical mixing and chemical bonding in current strategies are usually unstable and cannot effectively promote charge separation at their interfaces [5]. Therefore, the facile construction of stable hybrid photocatalysts with high photocatalytic activity is still of great significance.

Nonstoichiometric copper selenides (Cu2-xSe) can act as a good electron donor or acceptor with high ionic mobility in different hybrid materials, which exhibit good catalytic ability by facilitating electron transfer because of the high copper deficiency [28]. In this work, Cu2-xSe/porous gadolinium micro-networks (Cu2-xSe/P-MNs) hybrid photocatalysts are prepared by a one-step in-situ growth method using Cu2-xSe as the support. The prepared Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid photocatalysts have a wide absorption spectrum and high thermal stability. The degradation efficiency of rhodamine 6G (R6G) by using Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts can be as high as 99%, with a photocatalytic rate of up to 0.33 min−1 only under low-power (20 W) white light irradiation. The highly efficient photocatalysis is proved to mainly come from a ‘three channel’ catalytic path; that is, the photodegradation of R6G dyes is due to the oxidation of the h+, O2 •− and ⋅OH active species.

Copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O, 99%), selenium dioxide (SeO2, 99%), gadolinium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Gd(NO3)3·6H2O, 99%), R6G (95%) and ascorbic acid (99%) are obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid is from Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, GeminiSEM 500) is used to obtain the morphology of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs. UV-visible-near-infrared spectrophotometer (Lambda 950) is used to measure the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs. Lumine spectrophotometer (LS-55) is used to determine the fluorescence spectra of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi) is used to measure the elemental composition of Cu2-xSe and Cu2-xSe/P-MNs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer is used to measure the FT-IR spectrum of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs. Moreover, the Bruker ESR-300E spectrometer is used to obtain the electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra.

Nonstoichiometric Cu2-xSe of about 35 nm can be prepared by a seeded growth strategy using ascorbic acid as a reducing agent based on the previously reported method [29]. Then, Cu2-xSe nanoparticles are used as a support for the synthesis of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts. In detail, 0.4 g Gd(NO3)3·6H2O and 0.6 g citric acid are added into a clean autoclave, then dissolved in 5.0 mL aqueous solution containing 0.2 mg/mL Cu2-xSe. The above solution is further transferred into a 25 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 5 h. After the autoclave is cooled down naturally, the obtained porous Cu2-xSe/P-MNs materials are precipitated and suspended three times with ultrapure water. For comparison, solid gadolinium micro-networks (S-MNs) without porous structure are synthesized when only using Gd(NO3)3·6H2O (0.4 g) and citric acid (0.6 g) at 200 °C for 5 h. In the absence of Cu2-xSe, the prepared micro-networks have no porous structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation process of R6G using Cu2-xSe/P-MNs
ce:section.ce:para: Cu2-xSe/P-MNs (50 mg) are first added to the clean flask, and then different concentrations of H2O2 solution (1 mL) containing R6G solution (20 μM) are further dropped. The mixture solution is irradiated (light density is about 6 mW cm−2) under Xenon light (20 W) within 15 min. Finally, the fluorescence intensities of R6G are measured by using an excitation wavelength of 490 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Spherical nonstoichiometric Cu2-xSe are first synthesized by a facile hydrothermal approach based on the previous method [29]. The Cu2-xSe nanoparticles are homogeneous in size at approximately 35 nm (Figure S1). The prepared Cu2-xSe nanoparticles mainly contain carbon, oxygen, copper, and selenide elements based on the XPS characterization (Figure S2). The high-resolution Cu2p XPS spectrum (Figure S3) clearly shows that the binding energies of the Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 peaks at about 935.4 and 955.6 eV, respectively, which suggests that the Cu in the Cu2−xSe nanoparticles resides as either Cu + or Cu0 [30]. The Cu2p3/2 state shows the presence of two electronic states located at 934.8 eV and 935.7 eV (Figure S4). Because of the small binding energy shift, the distinction between Cu+ and Cu0 becomes difficult. Auger analysis is used to solve this problem based on the Auger parameter [15]. This strategy involves determining the kinetic energy for the Cu LMM spectrum (914.3 eV) and the binding energy for the Cu2p3/2 state (934.8 eV). The addition of the two energies corresponds to the known Auger parameter, and the value is about 1849.1, attributable to Cu+ in the first chemical state at 934.8 eV [31]. This result further confirms that Cu + exists in Cu2-xSe. In addition, two strong satellite peaks appear at around 940–945 eV and 960–965 eV (Figure S3), indicating the presence of a Cu2+ state [32]. These results show that nonstoichiometric Cu2-xSe are prepared. Subsequently, Cu2-xSe nanoparticles can act as good support for the in-situ growth of P-MNs materials. SEM image (Fig. 1 a) clearly reveals that Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid materials have a spatially well-defined 3D network structure of millimeter size. Moreover, the ‘node’ structure of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs is about 12 μm in size, and is well connected with stick-like junctions (Fig. 1b). Meanwhile, the XRD spectrum (Figure S5) shows that Cu2-xSe/P-MNs have a good crystalline structure, suggesting a good electron transfer ability during the photocatalytic process.

Importantly, the surface of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs contains abundant pore structure (Fig. 1c and d), which improves the surface area of this hybrid material and facilitates the adsorption of R6G dyes on the surface of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts. And the specific surface area of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs reaches 10.45 m2/g, measured by the N2 adsorption method (Figure S6). The formation of the porous structure is closely related to the Cu2-xSe because the precursors (citric acid and Gd(NO3)3·6H2O) in the absence of Cu2-xSe nanoparticles only can form the S-MNs without porous structure (Figure S7). In the absence of Cu2-xSe, Gd3+ cation can be well coordinated with the carboxyl and hydroxyl oxygen from the citric acid to form Gd3+ ions/citric acid complexes. Then, Gd3+ ions/citric acid complexes can be polymerized into S-MNs at high temperatures because the sresidual uncoordinated –COOH and –OH groups can be further coordinated with adjacent Gd3+ cation [33]. The introduction of Cu2-xSe can produce many porous structures in Cu2-xSe/P-MNs composites, which may be attributed to the release of water molecules that come from the dehydration reaction between the amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of Cu2-xSe and the carboxyl group of the S-MNs. The water molecules vaporize at high temperatures and result in the formation of a porous structure [34]. Therefore, S-MNs and P-MNs can be considered as the fluorescent aggregate of Gd3+ ions/citric acid complexes. Elemental mapping (Fig. 1e) shows that the prepared Cu2-xSe/P-MNs have carbon, oxygen, gadolinium, copper, and selenide atoms, and these elements are evenly distributed, indicating that the successful hybridization of Cu2-xSe and P-MNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composition and structure of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The composition and structure of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid photocatalysts are characterized in detail. XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 a) clearly reveals that Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid photocatalysts have carbon, oxygen, gadolinium, copper, and selenium elements, which further confirms the hybridization of Cu2-xSe and P-MNs. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) shows five peaks at about 284.5, 285.2, 286.4, 288.1 and 288.8 eV, which are attributed to C-C, C-O, C-O-C, C=O and O-C=O bonds, respectively. and the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) exhibits two peaks at about 531.3 and 532.1 eV, ascribed to C–OH and CO bonds, respectively. Moreover, the high-resolution Gd3d XPS spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows Gd3d5/2 and Gd3d3/2 peaks at about 1187.7 and 1220.0 eV, respectively [35]. Meanwhile, Gd4d5/2 and Gd4d3/2 binding energies in the high-resolution Gd4d XPS spectrum are located at about 142.6 and 148.9 eV, respectively, indicating that the oxidation state of Gd remains +3 in Cu2-xSe/P-MNs (Fig. 2e) [36]. The appearance of Gd3d and Gd4d peaks indicates that Gd exists in the form of Gd3+ in Cu2-xSe/P-MNs.

Furthermore, the high-resolution Cu2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2f) reveals that the binding energies of the Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2 peaks at about 933.4 and 953.2 eV, and no Cu2+ satellites are observed. This result shows that the Cu in Cu2-xSe/P-MNs mainly presents in either Cu0 or Cu + rather than Cu2+ [37]. The disappearance of Cu2+ should most likely be caused by the reduction of citric acid because citric acid is massively introduced in Cu2-xSe/P-MNs synthesis. Moreover, Fig. 2g shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Se3d with two peaks located at about 54.1 eV for Se3d5/2 and 54.9 eV for Se3d3/2, indicating the presence of Se2− ions [37]. Differing from the Cu2p spectrum, the Se3d spectrum of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs is basically consistent with that of Cu2-xSe (Figure S8), indicating that the chemical state of Se has not changed during the synthesis of Cu2-xSe/P-MN. These results clearly reveal that Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid materials can be well formed by the proposed one-pot in-situ growth approach.

FT-IR spectrum reveals that the Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid materials have abundant chemical groups. As shown in Fig. 2h, Cu2-xSe/P-MNs show characteristic absorption bands of O–H stretching vibrations at 3216 cm−1, C–O stretching vibrations at 1279 cm−1, C–O–C stretching vibrations at 1074 cm−1, respectively [38]. Moreover, two strong peaks appeared at 1388 and 1580 cm−1, which are directly attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of the carboxylate groups, suggesting the coordination between carboxyl groups and Gd3+ ions [33,39]. Importantly, the prepared Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid photocatalysts own good thermal stability (Fig. 2i). Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts have only little weight loss below 280 °C and have 66% weight when the temperature rises to 470 °C. Even if the temperature exceeds 600 °C, the Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts still have a weight of more than 50%. Therefore, the proposed Cu2-xSe/P-MNs photocatalysts show superior stability, which becomes the basis of good photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs under white light
ce:section.ce:para: The absorption spectra of Cu2-xSe and Cu2-xSe/P-MNs are first investigated. Results (Fig. 3 a) show that Cu2-xSe display well-defined absorption in the near-infrared region. The introduction of Cu2-xSe makes the strong absorption of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid materials extends from UV to near infrared, indicating a strong light-harvesting ability (Fig. 3a). Meanwhile, the optical band gap energy of Cu2-xSe is about 4.13 eV (Figure S9a). After the formation of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs, the bandgap energy can be reduced to 2.65 eV (Figure S9b). This low bandgap and high light-harvesting ability are responsible for the good photocatalytic activity of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs [40]. Fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3b) show that the fluorescent intensity of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs is significantly lower than that of S-MNs, indicating that the efficient transfer of photoexcited electrons from P-MNs to Cu2-xSe. Meanwhile, the electrons and holes generated by photoexcitation in Cu2-xSe/P-MNs can be effectively separated because of the hybridization of Cu2-xSe and P-MNs, which is strongly confirmed by the increased fluorescence lifetime from S-MNs (3.84 ns) to Cu2-xSe/P-MNs (4.98 ns) (Figure S10). On the basis of the above results, the hybridization of Cu2-xSe and P-MNs reduces the electrons and holes recombination rate, which effectively improves the photocatalytic activity of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid photocatalysts.

Furthermore, the photocatalytic mechanism of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs is investigated in detail. The radical scavenger experiment clearly shows the role of reactive oxygen species in the photocatalytic process of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the introduction of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, h+ scavenger) obviously inhibits the photocatalytic activity, suggesting that h+ plays an important role in the photodegradation of R6G dye. Meanwhile, isopropanol (IPA, ⋅OH scavenger) and p-benzoquinone (PBQ, O2 •− scavenger) can effectively reduce the photocatalytic activity of the Cu2-xSe/P-MNs, indicating that ⋅OH and O2 •− are also very important in the photodegradation of R6G dye. These results strongly confirm that h+, ⋅OH, and O2 •− are key reactive oxygen species in the photocatalytic process of R6G dye.

ESR spin-trapping technique is applied to further study the photocatalytic mechanism. Results show that a large number of ⋅OH and O2 •− formed in the photodegradation process (Fig. 4b and c). Through the above discussion, the photocatalytic process is described as follows:
(1) Cu2-xSe/P-MNs + white light → h+ + e−
(2) H2O2 + h+ → ⋅OOH + H+
(3) ⋅OOH → O2 •− + H+
(4) H2O2 + e− → ⋅OH + OH−

In conclusion, an in-situ growth strategy is developed for the preparation of Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid photocatalysts. Cu2-xSe/P-MNs have a strong and wide absorption capacity and can produce abundant photogenerated electrons and holes under low-power white light irradiation. Moreover, the photogenerated electrons and holes can be effectively separated because of the tight intimate interface between the two components of the hybrid photocatalyst. Therefore, Cu2-xSe/P-MNs hybrid photocatalysts show excellent photocatalytic activities and can completely degrade R6G within 15 min with a catalytic rate of up to 0.33 min−1. The h+, O2 •−, and ⋅OH are the main active species in the photocatalytic process, which provides a basis for ‘three channel’ catalysis. The good photocatalytic activities make Cu2-xSe/P-MNs be very promising for the treatment of environmental pollutants.

Qunwei Shu: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Wei Qiu: Visualization, Investigation, Writing. Min Luo: Investigation. Lihua Xiao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51776046), Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Foundation (No. 20191133), Youth Science and Technology Talent Development Fund in Guizhou Provincial Department of Education (No. 2018250), the Talents of High Level Scientific Research Fund in Guizhou Institute of Technology (No. XJGC20190934).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtsust.2022.100135.

Nanoscale

J. Phys. Chem. C

R., Assessing the role of Pt clusters on TiO2 (P25) on the photocatalytic degradation of acid blue 9 and rhodamine B. J. Phys. Chem. C 2020, 124, 8269-8278.

Environ. Sci.: Nano

Appl. Catal. B Environ.
